description: Rules that apply to ALL generated apps regardless of category.

guidelines.rule: The human user MUST be able to interact with the app. Never create an app that runs entirely on autopilot with no user input.
guidelines.why: Users repeatedly report that generated apps play themselves with no way for the human to participate. This is the #1 most common complaint.
examples: Game: user needs buttons to make decisions
Tool: user needs input fields and action buttons
Tracker: user needs add/edit/delete controls

guidelines.rule: All interactive elements (buttons, inputs, controls) must be large enough to click (minimum 44x44px touch target) and clearly visible.
guidelines.why: Generated apps often have tiny, hard-to-find controls that overlap or get pushed off-screen.
examples: Buttons should have padding: 10px 20px minimum
Don't put buttons inside scrollable areas that can be hidden

guidelines.rule: The app must have a clear visual indication of its current state. The user should always know what is happening and what they need to do next.
guidelines.why: Users get confused when the app state changes without feedback — they don't know if it's their turn, if something loaded, or if an error occurred.
examples: Show 'Your turn' indicator in games
Show loading states during async operations
Show success/error feedback after actions

guidelines.rule: Do NOT use placeholder implementations. Every function must have real, working logic. Never write empty function bodies, TODO comments, or stub code.
guidelines.why: LLMs under token pressure often produce skeleton code with placeholder functions that compile but do nothing.
examples: call() must actually deduct chips and advance the game
sort() must actually reorder the data

guidelines.rule: When iterating on existing code, preserve ALL working features. Never remove or break functionality that was working in the previous version.
guidelines.why: Feature regression is the #1 user complaint during iteration. New features often accidentally remove existing ones.
examples: If v1 had working card rendering, v2 must still render cards the same way or better

guidelines.rule: Interactive controls (buttons, inputs, sliders) must NEVER overlap with or be hidden behind other elements. Use proper layout with margins and padding so all controls are fully visible and clickable.
guidelines.why: Generated apps frequently have buttons positioned under cards, overlapping with other elements, or pushed off-screen. Users can't interact with controls they can't see or click.
examples: Fold/Call/Raise buttons must be in their own row BELOW the player's cards, not overlapping them
Use flexbox or grid with proper gap spacing
Controls should have at least 8px margin from adjacent elements

guidelines.rule: Use the CSS/SVG assets provided in the asset pack prompts. If a renderCard() function, card back SVG, felt texture CSS, or chip styles are provided, USE THEM. Do not reinvent simpler versions.
guidelines.why: Asset packs provide tested, polished visuals. LLMs often ignore injected assets and create inferior replacements.
examples: Use renderCard('A', '♠') for card faces, not plain text
Use the .chip CSS classes for chip displays
Use the felt gradient CSS for the table background

guidelines.rule: When a framework scaffold is injected (section starting '## Framework Scaffold:'), the scaffold's Package.swift / package.json / Cargo.toml / build.gradle is CANONICAL. Copy dependency URLs and versions VERBATIM. Do not bump or guess newer versions — the scaffold versions are vetted against real package registries.
guidelines.why: LLMs under pressure hallucinate non-existent versions (e.g., AudioKit 5.10.0 / 5.12.0 when actual latest is 5.7.2). This causes SPM/npm/cargo resolution failures and wastes build cycles. The scaffold's versions were verified to resolve and compile.
examples: If scaffold shows 'from: "5.6.0"' for AudioKit, copy '5.6.0' exactly — do not write '5.10.0' or '^5.0.0'

If scaffold lists AudioKitUI + SoundpipeAudioKit as dependencies, include BOTH in your Package.swift — do not drop any (they provide types like KeyboardView, MIDINoteNumber, Reverb that the scaffold code references)
If scaffold declares platforms: [.macOS(.v13)], match .v13 exactly — not .v15 or .v12
